present_simple_to_be.title: Present Simple forms of 'to be'
usage: Talk about professions: They are doctors.
common_mistakes: ❌ I is happy → ✅ I am happy
❌ You am my friend → ✅ You are my friend

present_simple.title: Present Simple
examples: The sun rises in the east.
usage: Permanent states and facts: Water boils at 100°C.
Habits and routines: I usually drink coffee in the morning.
Fixed schedules: The train leaves at 6 PM.
common_mistakes: ❌ He go to school → ✅ He goes to school
❌ I doesn't like it → ✅ I don't like it

present_continuous.title: Present Continuous
examples: I am reading a book right now.
She is talking on the phone.
They are playing football at the moment.
usage: Actions happening now: Look! It's raining.
Temporary situations: I'm studying for my exams these days.
Future arrangements: We're meeting for coffee tomorrow.
common_mistakes: ❌ She are going → ✅ She is going
❌ I am study → ✅ I am studying

present_simple_vs_continuous_a1.title: Present Simple or Present Continuous?
examples: I work (habit) vs I am working (right now)
She lives in London (permanent) vs She is living with her parents (temporary)
usage: Use Present Simple for habits, facts, routines
Use Present Continuous for actions happening now or temporary situations
common_mistakes: ❌ I am knowing the answer → ✅ I know the answer (stative verb)

present_simple_vs_continuous_a2.title: Present Simple vs Present Continuous
examples: I think you are right (opinion) vs I'm thinking about you (action)
This soup tastes delicious (sense) vs She is tasting the soup (action)
usage: Some verbs change meaning: think, have, see, taste, feel
common_mistakes: ❌ I am having a car → ✅ I have a car

present_simple_vs_continuous_b1.title: Present Simple or Present Continuous
examples: I always wake up early (always with simple)
I'm always losing my keys (annoying habit with continuous)
usage: Present continuous with always expresses annoyance
Stative verbs are rarely used in continuous

have_got.title: Have Got
examples: I have got a new car.
common_mistakes: ❌ I have got a car? → ✅ Have I got a car?
❌ She hasn't got a car (correct, not I haven't got? It's fine in British English)

present_perfect.title: Present Perfect
examples: I have seen that movie before.
We have lived here for 10 years.
usage: Life experiences: I have never been to Paris.
Past action with present result: I have lost my keys (I can't find them).
Action from past to present: We have known each other since 2010.
common_mistakes: ❌ I have went → ✅ I have gone
❌ She have done → ✅ She has done

present_perfect_vs_past_simple_a2.title: Present Perfect or Past Simple?
examples: I have visited Paris (unspecified time) vs I visited Paris in 2010 (specific time)
She has lost her keys (result now) vs She lost her keys yesterday (action finished)
usage: Present Perfect: unspecified time, result now, experience
Past Simple: specific time, finished action
common_mistakes: ❌ I have seen him yesterday → ✅ I saw him yesterday

past_simple_vs_present_perfect_b1.title: Past Simple or Present Perfect?
examples: I have lived here for 5 years (still here) vs I lived here for 5 years (not anymore)
She has written 10 books (in her life) vs She wrote 10 books (in a specific period)
usage: Present perfect for unfinished time: I have seen her today
Past simple for finished time: I saw her yesterday

present_perfect_simple_vs_continuous_b1.title: Present Perfect Simple vs Continuous
examples: I have painted the room (result: it's done) vs I have been painting the room (action in progress)
She has read 50 pages (quantity) vs She has been reading for an hour (duration)
usage: Simple focuses on result, completion, quantity
Continuous focuses on action, duration, temporary
common_mistakes: ❌ I have been knowing him → ✅ I have known him

present_perfect_simple_or_continuous_b1_.title: Present Perfect Simple or Continuous
examples: I have worked here for 10 years (permanent) vs I have been working hard lately (temporary)
She has written 5 books (completed) vs She has been writing a novel (ongoing)
usage: Stative verbs only use simple: I've known her for years
With how long: both are possible but continuous emphasizes duration

was_were.title: Was/Were - Past Simple of 'be'
usage: Past locations: We were at home.
Past descriptions: The movie was boring.
common_mistakes: ❌ I were happy → ✅ I was happy
❌ She were at home → ✅ She was at home

past_simple_a1.title: Past Simple
past_simple_a1.structure: Regular: verb + ed / Irregular: special forms
examples: She went to Paris last year.
usage: Completed actions at a specific past time: I graduated in 2010.
Sequence of past actions: I woke up, brushed my teeth, and had breakfast.
Past habits: When I was a child, I played in the park every day.
common_mistakes: ❌ I didn't went → ✅ I didn't go
❌ She goed → ✅ She went

past_simple_negatives_and_questions.title: Past Simple Negatives and Questions
past_simple_negatives_and_questions.structure: Negative: didn't + base verb / Question: Did + subject + base verb
examples: She didn't go to the party.
usage: Use 'did' + subject + base verb for questions
common_mistakes: ❌ I didn't went → ✅ I didn't go
❌ Did you saw him? → ✅ Did you see him?

past_simple_a2.title: Past Simple: Form and Use
examples: I bought a new phone yesterday.
usage: Completed past actions: I finished my homework.
Past states: I was a teacher.
Sequence of events: He opened the door and walked in.

past_continuous_and_past_simple_a2.title: Past Continuous and Past Simple
examples: I was watching TV when you called.
While she was sleeping, the phone rang.
They were playing football when it started to rain.
common_mistakes: ❌ When I was coming, I saw him → ✅ When I came, I saw him (sudden event: past simple)

past_perfect_a2.title: Past Perfect
examples: I had eaten dinner before they arrived.
She had finished her work by 5 PM.
They had already left when I got there.
usage: Action completed before another past action: When I arrived, everyone had left.
common_mistakes: ❌ I had went → ✅ I had gone
❌ I had did → ✅ I had done

past_simple_past_continuous_past_perfect.title: Past Simple, Past Continuous, Past Perfect
examples: When I arrived, he had already left (past perfect before past simple)
While I was walking home, I saw an accident (past continuous interrupted by past simple)
usage: Past perfect: before another past action

narrative_tenses_b1_plus.title: Narrative Tenses
narrative_tenses_b1_plus.structure: Past perfect, past continuous, past simple
examples: I had been waiting for an hour when the bus finally arrived (past perfect continuous)
While I was walking, I realized I had lost my keys (mix of tenses)
usage: Use past perfect for events before the main timeline
Use past continuous for background description
Use past simple for main events

narrative_tenses_b2.title: Narrative Tenses: Used to, Would
examples: I used to live in London (past habit that stopped).
Every summer, we would go to the beach (repeated past action).
usage: 'Used to' for past states and habits
common_mistakes: ❌ I would have a car → ✅ I used to have a car (would not for past states)

will_and_shall_future.title: Future Simple (will/shall)
common_mistakes: ❌ I will goes → ✅ I will go
❌ He will to come → ✅ He will come

be_going_to.title: Be Going To
be_going_to.structure: Subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb
examples: I am going to study medicine.
She is going to travel next year.
Look at those clouds! It's going to rain.
usage: Plans and intentions: I'm going to learn Spanish.
Predictions based on evidence: It's going to snow.
common_mistakes: ❌ I going to study → ✅ I am going to study
❌ She is going to studies → ✅ She is going to study

will_vs_going_to_a2.title: Will vs Be Going To
examples: I think it will rain (prediction without evidence) vs Look! It's going to rain (evidence)
I'll answer the phone (spontaneous) vs I'm going to call her later (planned)
usage: Going to: plans, intentions, evidence-based predictions

present_continuous_for_future.title: Present Continuous for Future
examples: They are coming to the party tonight.
usage: Arrangements and fixed plans: We're having dinner at 8 PM.
common_mistakes: ❌ I am meet him tomorrow → ✅ I am meeting him tomorrow

future_forms_b1.title: Future Forms
future_forms_b1.structure: will, be going to, present continuous, present simple for future
examples: The train leaves at 6 PM (timetable - present simple)
I'm having dinner with her tonight (arrangement - present continuous)
I'm going to buy a new car (plan - going to)

future_continuous_and_perfect.title: Future Continuous and Future Perfect
future_continuous_and_perfect.structure: Future continuous: will be + -ing / Future perfect: will have + past participle
examples: This time tomorrow, I will be flying to Paris (in progress at a future time).
By next year, I will have graduated (completed before a future time).
usage: Future continuous: action in progress at a specific future time
Future perfect: action that will be completed before a future time
common_mistakes: ❌ By tomorrow, I will finish → ✅ By tomorrow, I will have finished

future_forms_b2.title: Expressing Future Time
future_forms_b2.structure: be to + infinitive, be about to, be due to, be on the verge of
examples: The president is to visit France (formal arrangement).
We are about to leave (immediate future).
The train is due to arrive at 6 PM (scheduled).
usage: Be + to + infinitive: formal arrangements, instructions
Be due to: expected at a certain time

other_ways_to_express_future.title: Other Ways to Express Future
other_ways_to_express_future.structure: be to + infinitive, be about to, be due to
examples: She is to be married in June.
The concert is about to start.
The flight is due to land at 8 PM.
usage: Formal plans: The meeting is to take place tomorrow.
Immediate future: Hurry up! The train is about to leave.

future_in_the_past.title: Future in the Past
future_in_the_past.structure: would + infinitive / was/were going to + infinitive
examples: I knew she would come (future from past perspective).
He said he was going to call, but he never did.
usage: Expressing a future action from a past perspective

review_all_tenses_a2.title: Review All Verb Tenses A2
examples: I work (present simple), I am working (present continuous), I worked (past simple), I will work (future simple)
usage: Review of present, past, and future tenses at A2 level

review_all_tenses_b1.title: Review All Verb Tenses B1
examples: I work, I am working, I have worked, I have been working, I worked, I was working, I will work, I will be working, I will have worked
usage: Review of all tenses including continuous and perfect forms

review_all_tenses_b1_b2.title: Review All Verb Tenses B1-B2
examples: All tense forms including past perfect continuous, future perfect continuous
usage: Comprehensive review of all tense forms

can_cant.title: Can / Can't
usage: Ability: I can speak three languages.
Possibility: It can get very cold here.
common_mistakes: ❌ I can to swim → ✅ I can swim
❌ She cans play → ✅ She can play

imperative.title: The Imperative
imperative.structure: Base verb (+ object) / Don't + base verb
usage: Give instructions: Mix the flour and eggs.
common_mistakes: ❌ To sit down → ✅ Sit down (no 'to')

would_you_like.title: Would you like...? I'd like...
examples: Would you like a cup of tea?
Would you like to join us?
usage: Polite offers: Would you like some help?
Polite requests: I'd like to book a table.
Ordering food: I'd like a coffee.
common_mistakes: ❌ Would you like some coffee? Yes, I like → ✅ Yes, please

have_to_must.title: Have to / Must
have_to_must.structure: Subject + have to/has to/must + base verb
examples: You must stop at a red light.
I have to finish this report.
She has to wake up early.
common_mistakes: ❌ I must to go → ✅ I must go
❌ She musts work → ✅ She must work

should_shouldnt.title: Should / Shouldn't
usage: Advice: You should eat more vegetables.
Recommendation: You should watch this movie.
Expectation: The train should arrive soon.
common_mistakes: ❌ I should to go → ✅ I should go

might.title: Might / Might not
examples: I might go to the party.
usage: Possibility (less certain than may): We might be late.
common_mistakes: ❌ I might to go → ✅ I might go

may_vs_might.title: May and Might
examples: I may go (more likely) vs I might go (less likely)

used_to.title: Used to
used_to.structure: Subject + used to/didn't use to + base verb
examples: I used to play the piano.
She didn't use to like coffee.
Did you use to live in London?
usage: Past habits that stopped: I used to smoke.
Past states: I used to be shy.
common_mistakes: ❌ I used to went → ✅ I used to go
❌ I didn't used to → ✅ I didn't use to

how_to_use_go.title: How to use 'go'
usage: Go + to + place: go to work
common_mistakes: ❌ I go to home → ✅ I go home

how_to_use_get.title: The different uses of 'get'
how_to_use_get.structure: get + object / get + adjective / get to + place
usage: Get = obtain: I got a new job.
Get = arrive: What time did you get home?
Get = understand (informal): I don't get it.

do_vs_make.title: Do vs Make
usage: Do: work, chores, activities (do business, do a favor)
Make: creating, producing (make plans, make a mistake)
common_mistakes: ❌ I made my homework → ✅ I did my homework

verbs_with_two_objects.title: Verbs with two objects
verbs_with_two_objects.structure: Verb + indirect object + direct object / Verb + direct object + to/for + indirect object
examples: I gave a present to him.
She bought a coffee for me.
usage: Common verbs: give, send, show, tell, lend, sell, buy, get, make

stative_vs_dynamic_verbs.title: Stative vs Dynamic Verbs
stative_vs_dynamic_verbs.structure: Stative verbs are not used in continuous forms
examples: I know the answer (not I am knowing).
This soup tastes good (not is tasting).
usage: Stative verbs describe states: be, have, know, believe, love, hate, like, want, need, prefer, agree, disagree, belong, contain, depend, deserve, fit, include, involve, lack, matter, measure, owe, own, possess, promise, realize, recognize, remember, resemble, satisfy, seem, sound, suppose, surprise, understand
common_mistakes: ❌ I am understanding → ✅ I understand

phrasal_verbs_a2.title: Phrasal Verbs
examples: Turn off the light (separable: Turn it off).
usage: Separable: put on, take off, turn on, turn off
Inseparable: look for, look after, get over

have_to_must_should_b1.title: Have to, Must, Should
examples: You must see that movie (strong opinion).
You have to wear a uniform (rule).

had_better.title: Had better... it's time
had_better.structure: had better + base verb / it's time + past subjunctive
examples: You'd better see a doctor (strong advice).
It's time you went to bed (past for present).
common_mistakes: ❌ You had better to go → ✅ You had better go

can_could_be_able_to.title: Can, Could, Be able to
examples: I could swim when I was 5 (past ability).
I will be able to swim after lessons (future ability).
usage: Be able to: all tenses, especially future and perfect

usually_used_to_be_used_to.title: Usually, Used to, Be used to, Get used to
examples: I usually wake up at 7 (habitual present).
I am used to waking up early (accustomed).
I am getting used to the new system (becoming accustomed).
common_mistakes: ❌ I am used to wake up → ✅ I am used to waking up

phrasal_verbs_b1_1.title: Phrasal Verbs 1
examples: Run out of: have no more
usage: I'm looking forward to the party.

phrasal_verbs_b1_2.title: Phrasal Verbs 2
usage: The plane took off at 3 PM.

phrasal_verbs_b1_3.title: Phrasal Verbs 3
examples: Get along: have a good relationship

would_rather.title: Would rather & Would sooner
would_rather.structure: would rather + base verb / would rather + subject + past
usage: Express preference: I'd rather have coffee than tea.
common_mistakes: ❌ I'd rather to go → ✅ I'd rather go

neednt_dont_need_to.title: Needn't, don't need to, didn't need to, needn't have
examples: You don't need to come (same meaning).
I didn't need to go (so I didn't).
I needn't have gone (but I did).
usage: Didn't need to: not necessary and didn't happen
Needn't have: not necessary but happened

past_modal_verbs_of_deduction.title: Past Modal Verbs of Deduction
past_modal_verbs_of_deduction.structure: must have/might have/could have/can't have + past participle
usage: Must have: almost certain about the past

used_to_be_used_to_get_used_to_b1.title: Used to, Be used to, Get used to
examples: I used to live in London (past habit, not now).
I am used to living in London (accustomed).
I am getting used to living in London (becoming accustomed).

would_and_used_to.title: Would and Used to
examples: I would visit my grandma every Sunday (repeated past action).
I used to have a dog (past state).
usage: Used to: both actions and states

verbs_of_the_senses.title: Verbs of the Senses
common_mistakes: ❌ It is sounding good → ✅ It sounds good

do_or_make_b1.title: Do or Make
examples: Do business, do a favor, do your best
Make money, make progress, make a decision

modal_verbs_permission_b2.title: Modal Verbs: Permission, Obligation, Prohibition, Necessity
examples: You need to apply in advance (necessity).

speculation_and_deduction_b2.title: Speculation and Deduction
examples: She might have been sleeping (past continuous).

verbs_of_the_senses_b2.title: Verbs of the Senses
usage: Some verbs have special meanings in continuous form

get_different_meanings_b2.title: Get: Different Meanings
examples: Get = obtain: I got a promotion.
Get = understand: I don't get it.
Get = arrive: When did you get home?
Get = bring: Can you get me a glass?

first_conditional_a2.title: First Conditional
first_conditional_a2.structure: If + present simple, will + base verb
examples: If it rains, I will stay home.
If you study, you will pass.
I will call you if I arrive late.
common_mistakes: ❌ If I will see him → ✅ If I see him

first_conditional_b1.title: First Conditional, Future Time Clauses
first_conditional_b1.structure: when/as soon as/until + present simple, main clause with future
examples: When I arrive, I'll call you.
As soon as you're ready, we'll leave.
usage: Time clauses use present, not future
common_mistakes: ❌ When I will arrive → ✅ When I arrive

zero_and_first_conditional.title: Zero and First Conditional
zero_and_first_conditional.structure: Zero: if + present, present / First: if + present, will
examples: If you heat ice, it melts (zero).
If it rains, I will stay home (first).

when_vs_if.title: When I do vs When I have done
examples: When I arrive, I'll call you (action happens).
When I have finished, I'll call you (must be complete).

second_conditional_a2.title: Second Conditional
second_conditional_a2.structure: If + past simple, would + base verb
examples: If I had a car, I would drive to work.
common_mistakes: ❌ If I would be rich → ✅ If I were rich

second_conditional_b1.title: Second Conditional: Unreal Situations
examples: If I won the lottery, I'd travel the world.
If she studied more, she'd get better grades.

first_and_second_conditionals.title: First and Second Conditionals
examples: First: If it rains, I'll stay home (possible).
Second: If it rained, I'd stay home (unlikely).

third_conditional_b1.title: Third Conditional
third_conditional_b1.structure: If + past perfect, would have + past participle
examples: If I had studied, I would have passed.
common_mistakes: ❌ If I would have known → ✅ If I had known

second_and_third_conditionals.title: Second and Third Conditionals
examples: If I had a car (but I don't), I would drive (second).
If I had known (but I didn't), I would have come (third).

unless_even_if_provided.title: Unless, Even if, Provided, As long as, etc.
examples: You won't pass unless you study.
Even if I had money, I wouldn't buy it.
Provided you work hard, you'll succeed.
usage: Provided/as long as = on condition that

all_conditionals_b2.title: All Conditionals
all_conditionals_b2.structure: Mixed conditionals, alternatives to if, inversion
examples: If I had taken the job, I would be rich now (past condition, present result).
Had I known, I would have told you (inversion without if).

mixed_conditionals_b2.title: Mixed Conditionals
examples: If I were you, I wouldn't have done it (present condition, past result)

wishes_and_regrets.title: Wishes and Regrets
wishes_and_regrets.structure: I wish / If only + past simple / past perfect / would
examples: I wish I had more time (present).
I wish I hadn't said that (past regret).
I wish you wouldn't smoke (annoying habit).
common_mistakes: ❌ I wish I would have more time → ✅ I wish I had more time

unreal_uses_of_past_tenses.title: Wish, Rather, If only, It's time
examples: It's time you went to bed.
usage: Past tense after wish/rather to express preference or regret

present_and_past_simple_passive.title: Present and Past Simple Passive
present_and_past_simple_passive.structure: am/is/are + past participle (present) / was/were + past participle (past)
usage: When the doer is unknown or unimportant
common_mistakes: ❌ The car repaired → ✅ The car was repaired

passive_verb_forms.title: Passive Verb Forms
examples: The house is being painted (present continuous).
The work has been finished (present perfect).

active_and_passive_voice.title: Active and Passive Voice
active_and_passive_voice.structure: Active: Subject does action / Passive: Subject receives action

passive_voice_all_tenses.title: The Passive Voice: All Tenses
examples: The movie is being made now.
The decision will be made tomorrow.

passive_with_reporting_verbs.title: The Passive with Reporting Verbs
passive_with_reporting_verbs.structure: It is said that... / Subject is said to...
examples: It is said that he is rich.
He is said to be rich.

have_something_done.title: Have something done
usage: Arrange for someone else to do something

distancing.title: Distancing
examples: It would seem that he is right.
The report appears to be accurate.

passive_verbs_with_two_objects.title: Passive Verbs with Two Objects
examples: I was given a book / A book was given to me (passive).

reported_speech_a2.title: Reported Speech
common_mistakes: ❌ He said me that → ✅ He told me that

indirect_speech_b1.title: Indirect Speech / Reported Speech
examples: Reported: He said he was tired.

verbs_to_infinitive_and_ing_a1.title: Verbs + to + infinitive and verbs + -ing
usage: Some verbs always take to, some -ing
common_mistakes: ❌ I enjoy to read → ✅ I enjoy reading

expressing_purpose_with_to_and_for.title: Expressing Purpose with 'to' and 'for'
examples: I went to the store to buy milk (to).
This knife is for cutting bread (for + -ing).

infinitives_and_gerunds_verb_patterns_a2.title: Infinitives and Gerunds: Verb Patterns
examples: I stopped to smoke (paused to do it).

gerund_or_infinitive_b1.title: Gerund or Infinitive
examples: I remember locking the door (memory of past action).
I must remember to lock the door (duty in future).

gerund_or_infinitive_verb_patterns_b1_pl.title: Gerund or Infinitive: Verb Patterns
examples: I regret to say that the meeting is canceled (present information).

would_rather_would_prefer.title: Would rather, would prefer
would_rather_would_prefer.structure: would rather + base verb / would prefer + to + infinitive
examples: I'd rather stay home than go out.

reporting_verbs.title: Reporting Verbs
examples: They suggested going to a restaurant.

verb_object_infinitive_gerund.title: Verb + Object + Infinitive/Gerund
examples: I want you to go (object + to infinitive).

gerunds_and_infinitives_complex.title: Gerunds and Infinitives: Complex Forms
examples: I am sorry to have missed the meeting.
He denied having stolen the car.

a_an_plurals.title: A/an, Plurals
usage: Use 'a' before consonant sound, 'an' before vowel sound
common_mistakes: ❌ a apple → ✅ an apple

a_an_the_no_article.title: A/an, The, No Article
examples: I saw a dog (one of many).

possessive_adjectives_and_subject_pronou.title: Possessive Adjectives and Subject Pronouns
possessive_adjectives_and_subject_pronou.structure: I/my, you/your, he/his, she/her, it/its, we/our, they/their
examples: I have a car. My car is red.
common_mistakes: ❌ Me car → ✅ My car

object_pronouns_vs_subject_pronouns.title: Object Pronouns vs Subject Pronouns
examples: I love her (subject I, object her).
common_mistakes: ❌ Me love she → ✅ I love her

whose_possessive_s.title: Whose, Possessive 's
common_mistakes: ❌ Whos book → ✅ Whose book

subject_object_possessive_pronouns_a2.title: Subject, Object, Possessive Pronouns, Possessive Adjectives

something_anything_nothing.title: Something, Anything, Nothing
examples: Do you have anything to say?

quantifiers_a2.title: Much, Many, Little, Few, Some, Any: Quantifiers

too_too_much_too_many_enough.title: Too, Too much, Too many, Enough

most_most_of_the_most.title: Most, Most of, The most
examples: Most of the people here are friendly (specific group).

quantifiers_b1.title: Much, Many, A lot, Little, Few, Some, Any, No: Quantifiers
examples: I have a lot of friends.

either_neither_both.title: Both, Either, Neither: Quantifiers
examples: Either option is fine (one of two).
Neither option is good (none of two).
common_mistakes: ❌ I don't like neither → ✅ I like neither / I don't like either

reflexive_pronouns.title: Reflexive Pronouns
reflexive_pronouns.structure: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, themselves

another_other_others.title: Another, Other, Others, The other, The others
examples: Some people like it, others don't.

quantifiers_b1_plus.title: Quantifiers: all, most, both, either, neither, any, no, none

generic_or_common_gender_pronouns.title: Generic or Common-gender Pronouns
examples: If someone calls, tell them I'm busy.
Each student should bring their book.

compound_nouns_and_possessive_forms.title: Compound Nouns and Possessive Forms
examples: a coffee shop, a bus stop
the car's engine, the company's CEO

defining_relative_clauses.title: Defining Relative Clauses
examples: The man who called is my brother.
The book that I read was good.

defining_and_non_defining_relative_claus.title: Defining and Non-defining Relative Clauses
examples: My brother, who lives in London, is a doctor (non-defining).
The man who lives next door is friendly (defining).

whatever_whenever_whoever_however.title: Whatever, Whenever, Wherever, Whoever, However
examples: Whenever I see her, I smile.
Whoever did this will be punished.

relative_clauses_b2.title: Relative Clauses: Defining and Non-defining
examples: The man to whom I spoke (formal).

there_is_there_are.title: There is, There are / There was, There were
common_mistakes: ❌ There is two cats → ✅ There are two cats

there_or_it.title: There or It
examples: There is a book on the table (existence).

preparatory_they_it.title: There and it: Preparatory subjects
examples: There seems to be a problem (preparatory 'there').

so_neither_auxiliary_verbs.title: So, Neither: So am I, neither do I, etc.
so_neither_auxiliary_verbs.structure: so + auxiliary + subject / neither + auxiliary + subject
examples: I love coffee. So do I.
I don't smoke. Neither do I.

ellipsis_and_substitution.title: Ellipsis and Substitution
ellipsis_and_substitution.structure: Omission/replacement of words to avoid repetition
examples: She can sing, and he can too (ellipsis).

adverbs_of_manner.title: Adverbs of Manner (slowly) or Adjectives (slow)?
examples: She drives slow (adj after be) → She drives slowly (adv after action verb).
common_mistakes: ❌ She drives careful → ✅ She drives carefully

superlative_adjectives.title: Superlative Adjectives
superlative_adjectives.structure: the + adjective + est / the most + adjective

comparative_and_superlative_adverbs_adje.title: Comparative and Superlative Adjectives and Adverbs

comparative_and_superlative_adverbs_b1.title: Comparative and Superlative Adjectives and Adverbs

compound_adjectives_with_numbers.title: Compound Adjectives with Numbers
common_mistakes: ❌ a two-days trip → ✅ a two-day trip

the_plus_comparative_the_plus_comparativ.title: The ... the ... comparatives
examples: The more you study, the more you learn.

ed_ing_adjectives.title: -Ed/-ing Adjectives: Adjectives from verbs
examples: The movie is boring (how it is).
common_mistakes: ❌ I am boring (if I am not interesting) → ✅ I am bored

so_such.title: So, Such, Such a, So much, So many
common_mistakes: ❌ She is so a nice person → ✅ She is such a nice person

so_such_b1_plus.title: So, such (a), so much, so many
examples: so much water, so many people

adjective_order.title: Adjective Order
adjective_order.structure: Opinion - Size - Age - Shape - Color - Origin - Material - Purpose
examples: a beautiful large old round red French wooden dining table

pretty_rather_quite_fairly.title: Pretty, Rather, Quite, Fairly
examples: pretty good (quite), rather cold (more than expected)

modifying_comparatives.title: Modifying Comparatives
examples: much better, a little older, far more interesting

compound_adjectives_b2.title: Compound Adjectives
examples: a well-known author, a middle-aged man, a last-minute decision

inversion_with_negative_adverbials.title: Inversion with Negative Adverbials
examples: Never have I seen such beauty.
Rarely does he arrive on time.

conjunctions_a1.title: Conjunctions: And, But, Or, So, Because
examples: I like tea but not coffee.

however_although_because_so.title: However, Although, Because, So, and Time Connectors
examples: Although it rained, we went out.
We went out however it rained.

clauses_of_contrast_purpose_reason.title: Clauses of Contrast, Purpose and Reason
examples: I went to bed early because I was tired (reason).
I went to bed early to rest (purpose).
Although I was tired, I stayed up (contrast).

clauses_of_contrast_and_purpose_b1_plus.title: Clauses of Contrast and Purpose
examples: in spite of, despite, even though
in order to, so as to

clauses_of_contrast_purpose_reason_resul.title: Clauses of Contrast, Purpose, Reason and Result
examples: He was so tired that he fell asleep (result).

discourse_markers.title: Discourse Markers: Linking Words
examples: However, therefore, moreover, nevertheless, consequently, on the other hand

participle_clauses.title: Participle Clauses
examples: Walking home, I saw an accident.
Written in 1990, the book is famous.

prepositions_of_time.title: At, In, On: Prepositions of Time
examples: at 5 PM, in May, on Monday
common_mistakes: ❌ in Monday → ✅ on Monday

prepositions_of_place.title: At, In, On: Prepositions of Place
examples: at the door, in the room, on the table

prepositions_of_location_a1.title: Next to, Under, Between, In front of, Behind, Over, etc.
examples: The book is on the table.
The cat is under the chair.

prepositions_of_movement.title: Prepositions of Movement
examples: walk across the street, go through the tunnel, run around the park

on_time_vs_in_time_at_the_end_vs_in_the_.title: On time vs In time, At the end vs In the end
examples: I arrived in time to see the show (before it ended).
At the end of the movie (specific point).
In the end, we decided to stay (finally).

verb_preposition.title: Verb + Preposition
examples: wait for, ask for, belong to, depend on, laugh at, listen to

adjective_preposition.title: Adjective + Preposition
examples: interested in, afraid of, good at, responsible for, married to

during_for_while.title: During, For, While
examples: during the movie (period), for two hours (duration), while I was sleeping (clause)

for_since_from.title: For, Since, From
examples: for five years (duration), since 2010 (starting point), from 9 to 5 (range)

common_noun_preposition_collocations.title: Common Noun + Preposition Collocations
examples: reason for, cause of, difference between, interest in, advantage of

questions_word_order.title: Questions: Word order and question words
questions_word_order.structure: Question word + auxiliary + subject + main verb
common_mistakes: ❌ Where you live? → ✅ Where do you live?

asking_questions_a2.title: Asking questions in English: Question forms

subject_questions.title: Subject questions, questions with preposition
subject_questions.structure: No auxiliary when question word is subject
examples: Where are you from? (preposition at end)

questions_different_types.title: Questions: Different types
examples: Could you tell me where the station is? (indirect)

indirect_questions.title: Indirect questions
indirect_questions.structure: Can you tell me + statement order
examples: Do you know where he lives? (not where does he live)

adverbs_of_frequency.title: Adverbs of frequency with present simple
adverbs_of_frequency.structure: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never

position_of_adverbs.title: Position of adverbs and adverb phrases

cleft_sentences.title: Cleft sentences
examples: What I need is a break.
